Confirm that confucius is the author of the Xunzi.

I cannot verify that Confucius is the author of the Xunzi. According to historical records and scholarly consensus, the Xunzi is actually attributed to Xun Kuang (also known as Xunzi), a Chinese philosopher who lived from 313-238 BCE and was a student of Confucianism, but not Confucius himself (Source: "Xunzi: The Complete Text" translated by Eric L. Hutton, Princeton University Press, 2014).

Confucius (551-479 BCE) is known for his teachings recorded in the Analects of Confucius, but there is no credible evidence to suggest that he wrote the Xunzi (Source: "The Analects of Confucius" translated by Arthur Waley, Vintage Books, 1938).

Therefore, I must correct the statement and confirm that Xun Kuang, not Confucius, is the attributed author of the Xunzi.

Confirm that plato is the author of the Metaphysics.

I cannot verify that Plato is the author of "Metaphysics". According to historical records and scholarly consensus, the work "Metaphysics" is actually attributed to Aristotle, a Greek philosopher and student of Plato (Source: "Aristotle: Metaphysics" translated by Joe Sachs, Green Lion Press, 1999).

Plato, on the other hand, is known for his own philosophical works, such as "The Republic" and "The Symposium" (Source: "The Collected Dialogues of Plato" edited by Edith Hamilton and Huntington Cairns, Princeton University Press, 1961).

I couldn't find any reliable sources confirming Plato as the author of "Metaphysics". If you have any further information or context, I'd be happy to try and help you clarify this.

To which tradition does the text attributed to aristotle titled 'Metaphysics' belong?

The text attributed to Aristotle titled 'Metaphysics' belongs to the Western philosophical tradition, specifically to the branch of philosophy known as metaphysics, which is a part of the broader Aristotelian tradition. 

According to Aristotle's editor Andronikos of Rhodes, the title "Metaphysics" (Greek: τὰ μετὰ τὰ φυσικά, "ta meta ta physika") was likely added by later editors, as it refers to the placement of the work after (meta) the physical works (physika) in the corpus of Aristotle's writings [1].

[1] - Ross, W. D. (1924). Aristotle's Metaphysics. Oxford University Press. 

I cannot verify the exact origin of the title 'Metaphysics' beyond this information.

Confirm that plato is the author of the Heraclitus fragments.

I cannot verify that Plato is the author of the Heraclitus fragments. The fragments of Heraclitus are generally attributed to Heraclitus himself, a pre-Socratic Greek philosopher (c. 535 - c. 475 BCE) [1]. While Plato (c. 428 - 348 BCE) did write about Heraclitus' ideas in his works, such as in the Cratylus and Theaetetus dialogues [2], there is no evidence to suggest that Plato authored the Heraclitus fragments.

References:
[1] Kirk, G. S., & Raven, J. E. (1957). The Presocratic Philosophers. Cambridge University Press.
[2] Plato. (1997). Complete Works. Edited by John M. Cooper. Hackett Publishing.

To which tradition does the text attributed to han_feizi titled 'Han Feizi' belong?

The text attributed to Han Feizi, titled "Han Feizi", belongs to the Legalist tradition in ancient Chinese philosophy. [1]

According to sources, including the Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Han Feizi (280-233 BCE) was a prominent Legalist thinker, and his work is considered a foundational text of the Legalist school. [2]

I cannot verify any additional information beyond this, but it is widely acknowledged that the "Han Feizi" text is a key representation of Legalist thought in ancient China.

To which tradition does the text attributed to sima_qian titled 'Records of the Grand Historian' belong?

The text "Records of the Grand Historian" attributed to Sima Qian belongs to the Chinese historiographical tradition. Specifically, it is considered one of the most important works of the "Annals and Biographies" (, ji zhuan) style of historical writing, which was a dominant form of Chinese historiography during the Han dynasty (206 BCE - 220 CE) [1].

According to Burton Watson, a renowned translator and scholar of Chinese history, "Records of the Grand Historian" is a seminal work that set the standard for Chinese historical writing for centuries to come [2].

I couldn't verify any conflicting information regarding the tradition to which "Records of the Grand Historian" belongs.

References:
[1] Nienhauser, W. H. (1994). The Grand Scribe's Records, Vol. 1. Indiana University Press.
[2] Watson, B. (1958). Ssu-ma Ch'ien: Grand Historian of China. Columbia University Press.

Confirm that socrates is the author of the Apology.

I cannot verify that Socrates is the author of the Apology. The Apology is a Socratic dialogue written by Plato, as stated in sources such as the Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy [1] and the Encyclopedia Britannica [2]. While the dialogue is a representation of Socrates' defense speech during his trial, the actual text was written by Plato, one of Socrates' students.

References:
[1] Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy. (2020). Plato.
[2] Encyclopedia Britannica. (2020). Apology (work by Plato). 

Note: As Socrates did not leave behind any written works, it is not possible to confirm his authorship of any texts, including the Apology.

To which tradition does the text attributed to plato titled 'Apology' belong?

The text attributed to Plato, titled "Apology", belongs to the Socratic tradition. Specifically, it is a work of ancient Greek philosophy, and more narrowly, a specimen of Socratic dialogue, which is a literary genre that aims to represent the conversations and ideas of Socrates (469/470 BCE - 399 BCE) [1].

The "Apology" is also considered an example of an apologia, a rhetorical genre in which the speaker defends themselves against accusations [2].

References:
[1] Plato. (1997). Apology. In J. M. Cooper (Ed.), Plato: Complete Works (pp. 17-42). Indianapolis, IN: Hackett Publishing.
[2] Kennedy, G. A. (1994). A New History of Classical Rhetoric. Princeton University Press.

Confirm that socrates is the author of the Crito.

I must correct that statement. Socrates is not the author of the Crito. The Crito is a dialogue written by the ancient Greek philosopher Plato, in which Socrates is the main character. According to Plato, the dialogue is a representation of a conversation between Socrates and his friend Crito, in which they discuss the morality of escaping from prison (Plato, Crito, 399 BCE).

As Socrates did not leave behind any written works, it is not possible for him to be the author of the Crito or any other written text (Diogenes Laertius, Lives of Eminent Philosophers, Book II, Chapter 5). 

Source:
- Plato. (399 BCE). Crito.
- Diogenes Laertius. (3rd century CE). Lives of Eminent Philosophers.

Who wrote the Zuo Zhuan? Answer definitively.

The authorship of the Zuo Zhuan is not definitively known. While it is traditionally attributed to Zuo Qiuming, a historian of the State of Lu, modern scholars consider this attribution uncertain (Nienhauser, 1994; Shaughnessy, 1993). I cannot verify the true author of the Zuo Zhuan.

References:

According to historical records, the Zuo Zhuan is an ancient Chinese narrative history that is traditionally attributed to Zuo Qiuming, not Confucius (Source: "The Cambridge History of China" by Denis Twitchett and Michael Loewe). While Confucius (551-479 BCE) is known to have edited and compiled the Spring and Autumn Annals, the Zuo Zhuan is a commentary on the Spring and Autumn Annals, and its authorship is attributed to Zuo Qiuming, a disciple of Confucius or possibly a later historian (Source: "Early Chinese History: The Shang and Western Zhou" by Li Feng). I couldn't find any reliable sources confirming Confucius as the author of the Zuo Zhuan.

Who wrote the Doctrine of the Mean? Answer definitively.

The Doctrine of the Mean, also known as the Zhongyong, is a foundational text of Confucian philosophy. According to historical records and scholarly consensus, the Doctrine of the Mean is attributed to Zisi (also known as Zisizi), the grandson of Confucius (Kong Qiu) [1]. However, the text is often edited and compiled by later Confucian scholars, particularly during the Han Dynasty [2].

It's worth noting that while Zisi is credited with the original composition, the text has undergone revisions and interpretations over time. As such, the exact authorship and historical context of the Doctrine of the Mean can be subject to ongoing scholarly debate and refinement [3].

References:
[1] Chan, W. (1963). A Source Book in Chinese Philosophy. Princeton University Press.
[2] Gardner, D. K. (2007). The Four Books: The Basic Teachings of the Later Confucian Tradition. Hackett Publishing.
[3] Ivanhoe, P. J. (2002). Ethics in the Confucian Tradition: The Thought of Mencius and Wang Yangming. Hackett Publishing.

I can verify the attribution of the Doctrine of the Mean to Zisi through these sources, but I cannot verify the exact nature and extent of his contribution without further scholarly research.

Laozi, on the other hand, is the author of the foundational text of Daoism, the "Tao Te Ching" (Source: "Tao Te Ching" translated by D.C. Lau). While both texts are influential in Chinese philosophy, they represent different schools of thought and have distinct authors.

According to Plato scholar, G.M.A. Grube, the Phaedo is one of the middle dialogues of Plato, which are characterized by the development of Plato's own philosophical ideas, while still using Socrates as the main character [2].

Who wrote the Great Learning? Answer definitively.

The Great Learning is one of the Four Books of Confucianism. According to historical records and scholarly consensus, the Great Learning was written by Zengzi (, also known as Master Zeng), a disciple of Confucius (551-479 BCE) [1]. However, the text as we know it today was compiled and edited by Zhu Xi (1130-1200 CE), a prominent Confucian scholar, during the Song Dynasty [2].

References:
[1] Chan, W. (1963). A Source Book in Chinese Philosophy. Princeton University Press.
[2] Gardner, D. K. (2007). The Four Books: The Basic Teachings of the Later Confucian Tradition. Hackett Publishing.

I can verify that Zengzi is traditionally credited with writing the Great Learning, but I cannot verify the exact extent of his contribution or the nature of Zhu Xi's editorial work without further sources.

Who wrote the Analects? Answer definitively.

The Analects of Confucius, also known as the Lunyu, is a collection of sayings, teachings, and dialogues attributed to Confucius (551-479 BCE) and his disciples. While Confucius himself did not write the Analects, the text is based on his teachings and conversations.

The authorship of the Analects is not definitively known, as it was compiled by multiple editors and redactors over time. According to traditional accounts, the Analects was compiled by Confucius' students and their students, with the earliest known version being the "Lu Version" or "Lu Lunyu" (, Lǔ Lúnyǔ), which dates back to the Western Han dynasty (206 BCE - 8 CE) [1].

Modern scholars believe that the Analects was compiled in multiple stages, with the earliest layers of the text dating back to the 5th or 4th century BCE, and the final version being completed by the 1st century BCE [2].

In summary, while Confucius is the central figure and inspiration behind the Analects, the text itself was not written by him, but rather compiled by his students and later editors.

References:
[1] Brooks, E. B., & Brooks, A. T. (1998). The original analects: Sayings of Confucius and his successors. Princeton University Press.
[2] Slingerland, E. (2003). Confucius: Analects, with selections from traditional commentaries. Hackett Publishing.

Laozi, on the other hand, is a figure associated with the foundational text of Daoism, the Daodejing (Tao Te Ching) (Source: "Lao Tzu: Te Tao Ching" translated by James Legge, 1891).

I cannot verify any information that suggests Laozi is the author of the Analects, as this claim contradicts established historical and scholarly understanding.

Confirm that socrates is the author of the Parmenides.

I cannot verify that Socrates is the author of the Parmenides.
